planting.note: Direct sow in fall; cold stratification over winter improves germination rates in spring.

directions: A tall, bold native ironweed of moist prairies, floodplain meadows, and roadsides of the central US, producing flat-topped clusters of intensely vivid, deep purple composite flowers from August through October on upright, stout stems with lance-shaped, somewhat rough-textured leaves. The electric-purple flower color is among the most striking of any native North American wildflower. Direct sow in fall in moist, average to rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; plants spread slowly by rhizomes once established. Excellent as a tall background plant in native prairie gardens, providing late-season nectar for monarchs, native bees, and swallowtail butterflies.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall on calcareous or limestone-derived soils; cold exposure over winter aids germination.

directions: A native ironweed of moist, open, calcareous prairies, limestone glades, and stream margin meadows in Arkansas, Oklahoma, and Missouri, bearing clusters of deep purple-violet composite flowers in late summer and fall on upright stems with narrow, lance-shaped, rough leaves. More compact and tolerant of calcareous soils than Vernonia missurica, making it especially well suited to prairie restorations on limestone-derived substrates. Grow in moist, average to slightly alkaline, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun; direct sow in fall. An outstanding late-season nectar source for monarchs and native bees.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into dry, sandy, infertile soil in full sun; avoid amended or fertile soils.

directions: A distinctive, low-growing native ironweed that appears nearly stemless, with large, broadly elliptic, rough-textured basal leaves lying flat to the ground and short upright flowering stems bearing clusters of deep purple composite flowers in summer. Native to dry, sandy, open pine flatwoods and barrens of the southeastern coastal plain from Virginia to Florida, it is much shorter and more drought-tolerant than other Vernonia species. Grow in dry, sandy, infertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; rich or amended soils cause rank growth and reduce flowering. An excellent choice for sandy native gardens and longleaf pine flatwood restorations in zones 6-9.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist to wet, sandy, acidic soil; keep consistently moist throughout establishment.

directions: A delicate native perennial of wet longleaf pine savannas, seepage bogs, and flatwood margins of the southeastern coastal plain, producing narrow, grass-like basal leaves and slender upright flowering stems topped with solitary, fluffy, pink to rose-lavender, powder-puff composite flower heads in late summer to fall. One of several Marshallia species endemic to southeastern US flatwoods and among the most visually charming of the group. Grow in moist to wet, sandy, acidic, nutrient-poor soil in full sun; avoid fertile or amended soils, which suppress flowering and favor rank vegetative growth. An excellent, underused native wildflower for wet garden edges and longleaf pine savanna restorations in zones 7-9.

planting.note: Plant nursery-propagated stock in spring; wild collection is prohibited given the species' rarity.

directions: A rare, conservation-listed native Marshallia endemic to mafic rock outcrops and open rocky woodlands in a small area of the Piedmont of North Carolina and Virginia, producing broad, spoon-shaped basal leaves and slender stems topped with solitary pink to rose-purple, powder-puff composite flower heads in summer. The mafic substrate of its native habitat gives rise to highly specialized, nutrient-limited plant communities with few close parallels in cultivation. Grow in moderately well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in partial to full sun; purchase only nursery-propagated stock from specialty native plant nurseries, as planting cultivated specimens directly supports conservation of this geographically restricted endemic.
